children.label: Security Offerings and Capital Markets Law

candidates.label: Corporate Issuer
candidates.definition: A Corporate Issuer refers to a corporation that issues debt or equity securities to raise capital for various purposes, such as expanding operations, financing acquisitions, or refinancing existing debt. These securities can include corporate bonds, stocks, and commercial paper, and are sold to investors in the capital markets.

candidates.label: Mergers and Acquisitions Law
candidates.definition: Law dealing with companies that are purchasing and/or merging with other companies.

candidates.label: Corporate Law
candidates.definition: Law that governs the formation and operation of corporations.

candidates.label: Mergers and Acquisitions Documents
candidates.definition: Mergers and Acquisitions Documents are written or recorded representations of agreements and disclosures related to the purchase or merger of companies.

hierarchy_path.label: Document Collections by Areas of Law

candidates.label: Merger Agreement
candidates.definition: A Merger Agreement is a legal document that outlines the terms and conditions of the merger between two or more companies.

hierarchy_path.label: Document Collections by Areas of Law

candidates.label: Document Collections by Areas of Law
candidates.definition: Document Collections by Areas of Law are a type of Transactional Document that group together various legal documents related to specific areas of law, such as Real Estate, Mergers & Acquisitions, and Trusts and Estate Planning.
synonyms: Colecciones de documentos por áreas del derecho
Coleções de documentos por áreas do direito
Collections de documents par domaines de droit
Document Collections by Areas of Law

candidates.label: Mergers and Acquisitions Events
candidates.definition: Mergers and Acquisitions (M&A) Events refer to the specific events and phases involved in the process of merging or acquiring one company by another, including the due diligence and closing events.

candidates.label: Transaction Events
candidates.definition: Events occurring during the course of a transaction.

candidates.label: Merger
candidates.definition: The concept of "Merger" refers to a type of M&A deal structure where two or more companies combine into a single entity, with one surviving entity and the others being absorbed.

candidates.label: Mergers and Acquisitions Objectives
candidates.definition: Mergers and Acquisitions (M&A) Objectives refer to the specific aims, goals, plans, and intentions constructed by parties involved in a merger or acquisition transaction, including deal structure, deal size, governing law, jurisdiction, deal protection, closing conditions, remedies, consideration, target industry, and material adverse effect (MAE) language.

candidates.label: Friendly Takeover
candidates.definition: A friendly takeover involves the acquiring company working in cooperation with the target company's management and board of directors to negotiate and agree upon the terms of the acquisition. In a friendly takeover, both parties collaborate to ensure a smooth transition and integration of the businesses.

candidates.label: Merger Clause
candidates.definition: A merger clause is a contractual clause that specifies that the written agreement represents the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes any prior or contemporaneous oral or written agreements or understandings.

candidates.label: Merger/Acquisition No-Vote
candidates.definition: A Merger/Acquisition No-Vote is a concept in which a breakup fee is triggered if the shareholders of a company do not approve a proposed merger or acquisition.
synonyms: Voto en contra de la fusión/adquisición

candidates.label: Transactional Objectives
candidates.definition: Transactional Objectives are specific aims, goals, plans, or intentions related to a legal transaction, such as a merger or acquisition, and the frameworks that support their execution.
synonyms: トランザクション目標

candidates.label: M&A Remedies
candidates.definition: M&A Remedies refer to the legal actions or provisions that are put in place to address issues that arise during or after a merger or acquisition, such as breaches of contract or failure to meet agreed-upon terms.
synonyms: Remèdes en matière de fusions et acquisitions

candidates.label: Control Position
candidates.definition: An M&A Control Position refers to a stake in a company acquired through mergers and acquisitions that gives the holder significant influence over corporate decisions. This control is often achieved by obtaining a majority of voting shares or a significant minority stake sufficient to influence board decisions.

candidates.label: Forward Merger
synonyms: フォワードマージャー

candidates.label: Mergers and Acquisitions Practice
candidates.definition: The purchase of one company by another (an acquisition), or the blending of two companies into a new entity (a merger). A merger is a process by which two companies join and one new company continues to exist.

candidates.label: Transactional Practice
candidates.definition: Any process that involves two or more parties in creation of a contract.
synonyms: トランザクション実務

candidates.label: Board Secretary
candidates.definition: A Board Secretary, also known as Secretary of the Board, is an officer responsible for managing the administrative tasks of a board of directors, including preparing agendas, recording meeting minutes, and maintaining corporate governance documents. The Secretary ensures compliance with legal and regulatory requirements and facilitates communication between the board and executive management.

candidates.label: Institutional Investor
candidates.definition: Entities that pool together funds to purchase securities, real property, and other investment assets.

candidates.label: Corporate Representative Witness
candidates.definition: A Corporate Representative Witness is a witness who testifies on behalf of a corporation in a legal matter, providing information about the corporation's policies, practices, or actions.

candidates.label: Corporate Law Department
candidates.definition: A Corporate Law Department is a division within a business entity (e.g., corporation, LLC, partnership) responsible for handling legal matters and ensuring compliance with laws and regulations. It advises the entity on legal issues, drafts and reviews legal documents, and represents the corporation in legal proceedings.

candidates.label: Corporate Governance Law
candidates.definition: Mechanisms, processes, and relations by which corporations are controlled and operated.

candidates.label: Corporate Law
candidates.definition: Law that governs the formation and operation of corporations.

candidates.label: Corporate Insolvency Law
candidates.definition: Law relating to insolvency or reorganization of corporations.

candidates.label: Corporate Articles
candidates.definition: Corporate Articles, also known as Articles of Incorporation, are legal documents filed with a government body to officially establish a corporation. They outline fundamental information such as the corporation's name, purpose, and stock structure.

candidates.label: Corporate Disclosure Statement
candidates.definition: A Corporate Disclosure Statement is a document filed by a corporation in a legal proceeding that provides information about the corporation's ownership, affiliates, and potential conflicts of interest.

candidates.label: Charter
candidates.definition: A Charter is a formal document that establishes an organization, specifying its purpose, structure, and basic operating principles. It sets the entity's foundational governance structure.

candidates.label: Bylaws
candidates.definition: Bylaws are internal regulations adopted by an organization to manage its operations and procedures. They detail roles of officers, meeting protocols, and other organizational rules.

candidates.label: Corporate Representative Deposition
candidates.definition: A Corporate Representative Deposition is a legal proceeding where a company designates a representative to testify on its behalf in response to a subpoena or other legal request for information.

candidates.label: Governance Risk
candidates.definition: Governance Risk refers to the potential for negative consequences arising from an organization's ineffective or inadequate leadership, decision-making processes, and oversight structures. This can result from poor corporate governance practices, lack of transparency, or weak accountability mechanisms, ultimately leading to reputational damage, legal liabilities, and decreased stakeholder trust, which can impact the organization's performance and sustainability.

candidates.label: Accountability Principle
candidates.definition: Accountability is the privacy principle that a data controller should be accountable for complying with measures which give effect to the principles stated above.

candidates.label: Corporate Governance Analysis
candidates.definition: Corporate Governance Analysis refers to the examination and evaluation of the internal policies, procedures, and practices of a corporation to ensure compliance with legal and regulatory requirements, and to promote transparency and accountability.

candidates.label: Service
candidates.definition: The legal work performed, usually by a Legal Services Provider, in the course of a legal matter.

candidates.label: Corporate Representative Deposition Practice
candidates.definition: The practice of conducting a deposition of a corporate representative, who is designated to testify on behalf of the corporation, in a legal matter.
synonyms: Pratique de la déposition des représentants d'entreprise
Práctica de deposición de representantes corporativos
Prática de depoimento de representante corporativo

candidates.label: Corporate Campaigns Practice
candidates.definition: Corporate Campaigns Practice involves the development and execution of strategy and strategic communication efforts aimed at influencing corporate policies and practices. This practice includes managing campaigns that address issues such as employee rights, environmental sustainability, and corporate social responsibility to achieve specific advocacy goals.

candidates.label: Corporate President
candidates.definition: A Corporate President is a senior executive often responsible for overseeing the day-to-day operations of a company and implementing strategic initiatives set by the board of directors. The President often acts as the second-highest-ranking officer in a company, reporting directly to the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) or, in some cases, the board.

candidates.label: Claims Agent
candidates.definition: A claims agent is a person who acts on behalf of an individual or organization to manage and process insurance claims.
synonyms: クレームエージェント

candidates.label: Trustee
candidates.definition: A trustee is an individual person or member of a board given control or powers of administration of property in trust with a legal obligation to administer it solely for the purposes specified.

candidates.label: Law of Obligations
candidates.definition: The Law of Obligations in civil law systems refers to a set of rules governing the duties, responsibilities, and rights of parties engaged in contractual or quasi-contractual relationships. It provides the legal framework for various types of obligations, such as contractual obligations, torts, and unjust enrichment, specifying how these obligations arise, are performed, or can be extinguished.

candidates.label: Financial Obligations
candidates.definition: Financial Obligations (Financial Liabilities) refer to any monetary commitments or liabilities an individual or entity is responsible for, such as paying off debts, fulfilling contract terms, or covering recurring expenses. These obligations can be short-term or long-term and failing to meet them could result in financial penalties or legal consequences

candidates.label: Warranty Obligations
candidates.definition: Warranty Obligations, also known as Warranty Liabilities, are commitments made by a company to repair, replace, or refund products that are found to be defective within a specified period. These obligations are recognized as a liability on the company's balance sheet, and an expense is recorded in the income statement to match the estimated future costs of honoring the warranties.

candidates.label: Environmental Obligations
candidates.definition: Environmental Obligations, also known as Environmental Liabilities, refer to a company's legal responsibilities for the remediation or restoration of environmental damage, often due to its operations or business practices. These obligations are recorded as liabilities on the company's balance sheet and can include costs for cleanup, legal penalties, and ongoing monitoring, which may be significant and long-term.

candidates.label: Lease Obligations
candidates.definition: Lease Obligations refer to the financial and contractual responsibilities that a lessee must fulfill as part of a lease agreement. These often include timely payment of rent, adherence to maintenance and use provisions, and compliance with any terms for lease termination or renewal

candidates.label: Military Court
candidates.definition: A Military Court is a specialized court within the jurisdiction of the military that has the authority to hear and decide cases involving military personnel and offenses related to military service.

candidates.label: Trust
candidates.definition: A trust, in estate planning, is a legal entity that holds and manages assets for the benefit of specific individuals or entities, known as beneficiaries. It's established by a grantor (or settlor) and is overseen by a trustee who ensures the assets are managed and distributed according to the grantor's instructions.

candidates.label: Fiduciary Breaching Duty
candidates.definition: The failure of a fiduciary to fulfill their legal obligation to act in the best interest of their client or beneficiary.

candidates.label: Breach of Fiduciary Duty
candidates.definition: Breach of Fiduciary Duty refers to a situation where a person in a position of trust and confidence, such as a trustee, agent, or business partner, fails to act in the best interests of the other party and instead acts in their own self-interest, resulting in harm or loss to the other party.

candidates.label: Employee Breach of Fiduciary Duty
candidates.definition: An Employee Breach of Fiduciary Duty refers to a situation where an employee violates their legal obligation to act in the best interest of their employer by engaging in actions that benefit themselves or a third party, resulting in harm to the employer.
synonyms: Incumplimiento de deber fiduciario del empleado
Verletzung der Treuepflicht durch den Arbeitnehmer
Violation du devoir de fidélité de l'employé

candidates.label: Breach of Fiduciary Duty by Trustee
candidates.definition: The breach of fiduciary duty by trustee refers to a situation where a trustee fails to act in the best interest of the beneficiaries of a trust, violating their legal obligation to manage the trust assets and affairs with loyalty, honesty, and good faith.

candidates.label: Fiduciary Duty
candidates.definition: Fiduciary duty is a legal obligation requiring one party, the fiduciary, to act in the best interest of another party, typically a beneficiary or principal, with loyalty and care. This duty is paramount in relationships involving trust and confidence, such as those between trustees and beneficiaries, corporate directors and shareholders, or financial advisors and clients.

candidates.label: Obligation of Contracts Clause Claim
candidates.definition: An Obligation of Contracts Clause claim is a legal argument asserting that a law or government action violates the Obligation of Contracts Clause of the United States Constitution by impairing the obligations of existing contracts, without a legitimate public purpose, thereby infringing on the sanctity and enforceability of contractual rights.
synonyms: Anspruch aus der Klausel zur Verpflichtung von Verträgen
Reclamación de la Cláusula de Obligación de Contratos
Reivindicação de cláusula de obrigação contratual
Réclamation de clause d'obligation de contrat

candidates.label: Civil Claims
candidates.definition: Civil Claims are legal actions brought by one party against another for a non-criminal matter, such as a breach of contract or personal injury, and seek monetary damages or other relief.

candidates.label: Obligation
candidates.definition: An obligation is a legal or moral duty that requires a certain course of action or behavior, often enforceable by law or ethical principles.

candidates.label: Breach of Contract Claims
candidates.definition: A breach of contract claim is a legal action taken by one party against another for failing to fulfill their obligations as outlined in a contractual agreement.
synonyms: Actions en cas de violation de contrat

candidates.label: Obligation Analysis
candidates.definition: Obligation Analysis is the process of examining and evaluating the legal responsibilities and duties of a debtor in a bankruptcy or financial restructuring matter, including the analysis of various considerations such as vendor agreements, utilities, securities reporting, liens, financial reports, insurance, cash usage, debt agreements, dischargeability, employee benefits, pensions, and environmental issues.

candidates.label: Director Agreement
candidates.definition: A Director Agreement is a contract between a corporation and its directors that stipulates the terms of their relationship, such as duties, compensation, and any confidentiality or non-compete clauses.

candidates.label: Antidumping Duty Payment
synonyms: An Antidumping Duty Payment refers to the payment made by importers to the government as a result of antidumping duties imposed on imported goods sold below fair market value. These payments are collected to offset the effects of dumping and protect domestic industries from unfair competition.

hierarchy_path.label: Specific Goods and Services Tax Payment

candidates.label: Military Reserve Duty
candidates.definition: Military Reserve Duty refers to the service in a military reserve force, which is composed of citizens who undergo training but typically serve part-time. Members of the reserve may be called to active duty during emergencies, conflicts, or other situations requiring additional manpower, and are otherwise obligated to participate in periodic training exercises.

candidates.label: Countervailing Duty Payment
candidates.definition: A Countervailing Duty (CVD) Payment refers to the payment made by importers to the government as a result of countervailing duties imposed on imported goods that benefit from foreign government subsidies. These payments aim to counteract the effects of such subsidies, ensuring fair competition for domestic producers.

hierarchy_path.label: Specific Goods and Services Tax Payment

candidates.label: Military Active Duty
candidates.definition: "Military Active Duty" refers to a period during which individuals in the armed forces are in full-time service, actively engaged in their duties, and can be deployed at any time. This status contrasts with reserve or part-time duties and involves more direct involvement in military operations.

candidates.label: Customs and Import Duty Payment
candidates.definition: Customs and Import Duty Payment refers to taxes, stamp duties, and surcharges levied exclusively on imported products. This includes specific levies on agricultural imports in European Union member countries and payments under the Monetary Compensation Accounts (MCA) system. Customs duties collected by EU member states on behalf of the European Union are also reported under this heading. These taxes are distinct from general goods and services taxes or excises that apply to both imported and domestically produced goods.

hierarchy_path.label: Specific Goods and Services Tax Payment

candidates.label: Trust, Fiduciary, and Custody Activities
candidates.definition: This U.S. industry comprises establishments primarily engaged in providing trust, fiduciary, and custody services to others, as instructed, on a fee or contract basis, such as bank trust offices and escrow agencies (except real estate).

candidates.label: Heavy Duty Truck Manufacturing
candidates.definition: This industry comprises establishments primarily engaged in (1) manufacturing heavy duty truck chassis and assembling complete heavy duty trucks, buses, heavy duty motor homes, and other special purpose heavy duty motor vehicles for highway use or (2) manufacturing heavy duty truck chassis only.

candidates.label: Fiduciary Duty
candidates.definition: Fiduciary duty is a legal obligation requiring one party, the fiduciary, to act in the best interest of another party, typically a beneficiary or principal, with loyalty and care. This duty is paramount in relationships involving trust and confidence, such as those between trustees and beneficiaries, corporate directors and shareholders, or financial advisors and clients.

candidates.label: Duty of Loyalty
candidates.definition: Duty of loyalty is a legal obligation that requires fiduciaries to act in the best interests of their beneficiaries or principals, avoiding conflicts of interest and self-dealing. This duty ensures that fiduciaries prioritize the interests of those they serve above their own personal gain.

candidates.label: Fiduciary Relationship
candidates.definition: A fiduciary relationship is a legal relationship in which one party is entrusted with the care and management of another party's assets or interests, and is obligated to act in the best interests of the other party, with loyalty, honesty, and good faith.

candidates.label: Duty of Care
candidates.definition: Duty of care is a legal obligation requiring individuals and organizations to adhere to a standard of reasonable care while performing acts that could foreseeably harm others. This duty ensures that actions or omissions do not result in injury or damage to other parties.

candidates.label: Breach of Fiduciary Duty

candidates.definition: An expert in the area of securities, including stocks and investments.

candidates.label: Securities and Financial Instruments Law
candidates.definition: Law covering transactions and other dealings with securities including both federal- and state-level regulation by purely governmental regulatory agencies, listing requirements of exchanges, and rules of self-regulatory organizations.

candidates.label: Treasury Securities
candidates.definition: Treasury securities are debt instruments issued by a government to raise funds and are backed by the full faith and credit of that government. They come in various forms, including Treasury bills, notes, and bonds, each with different maturity lengths and yield characteristics, and are considered among the safest investments due to their low default risk

candidates.label: Asset-Backed Securities
candidates.definition: Asset-Backed Securities (ABS) are financial instruments backed by a pool of assets, typically loans or receivables, that generate cash flows to pay interest and principal to investors. ABS allow lenders to transfer the risk of the underlying assets to investors and are commonly used to improve liquidity and raise capital.

candidates.label: Mortgage-Backed Securities
candidates.definition: Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS) are investment vehicles that represent claims to the cash flows from pools of mortgage loans, primarily residential or commercial properties. Investors in MBS receive periodic payments derived from the principal and interest of the underlying mortgages, offering a means to invest in real estate without direct property ownership.

candidates.label: Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities
candidates.definition: Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS) are a type of asset-backed security backed by a pool of commercial real estate loans, such as those for office buildings, hotels, shopping centers, and other income-generating properties. Investors in CMBS receive payments from the principal and interest collected on the underlying commercial mortgages, offering exposure to the commercial real estate market.

candidates.label: Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities

candidates.definition: A Securities Lending Agreement allows the temporary transfer of securities, typically stocks or bonds, from an initial holder to another party in return for collateral, which could be cash or other securities. candidates.label: Exempt Securities Notification
candidates.definition: An Exempt Securities Notification is a notice of exempt offering of securities filed with the financial regulator (e.g., U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)) by companies that have sold securities without registration, in reliance on an exemption (e.g., provided by Regulation D or Section 4(a)(5) of the Act). candidates.definition: The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is an independent agency of the United States federal government. candidates.label: Merger
candidates.definition: The concept of "Merger" refers to a type of M&A deal structure where two or more companies combine into a single entity, with one surviving entity and the others being absorbed.

candidates.label: Post-Closing Covenants
candidates.definition: "Post-Closing Covenants" are promises or obligations that parties agree to adhere to after the completion of a transaction, such as a merger. In merger agreements, these covenants typically address ongoing commitments, actions, or restrictions to ensure a smooth transition and integration, or to protect the interests of the involved parties after the deal has closed.

candidates.label: Completion Accounts
candidates.definition: Completion Accounts are financial statements prepared as of the closing date of an M&A transaction to determine the final purchase price. They include adjustments for working capital, cash, debt, and other relevant items, ensuring the buyer pays a price reflecting the target's actual financial position at closing.

candidates.label: Merger of Idea and Expression
candidates.definition: The Merger of Idea and Expression doctrine in copyright law applies when an idea and its expression are so intertwined that the idea cannot be expressed in any other way, making the expression non-protectable. This principle recognizes that when only one or very few ways exist to express an idea, protecting such expression would effectively grant a monopoly over the idea itself.

candidates.label: Post-Judgment Interest
candidates.definition: Post-Judgment Interest refers to the interest that accrues on a monetary award after a judgment has been entered by a court.

candidates.label: Forward Merger
synonyms: フォワードマージャー

candidates.label: One-Step Merger
candidates.definition: A One-Step Merger is a type of merger where the acquiring company merges directly with the target company, without the need for a separate shareholder vote on the merger agreement.

candidates.label: Forward Sub Merger
synonyms: フォワードサブマージャー

candidates.label: Post-Grant Review
candidates.definition: A Post-Grant Review (PGR) is a type of administrative dispute proceeding practice conducted by the U.S. Patent Trial and Appeal Board (PTAB) to review the validity of a granted patent.

candidates.definition: Law surrounding business competition.

candidates.label: Petition
candidates.definition: A petition is a formal written request submitted to a court or other authority, typically seeking a specific legal action or remedy.

candidates.definition: Comm. Ct.

candidates.label: Trust
candidates.definition: A trust, in estate planning, is a legal entity that holds and manages assets for the benefit of specific individuals or entities, known as beneficiaries. It's established by a grantor (or settlor) and is overseen by a trustee who ensures the assets are managed and distributed according to the grantor's instructions.

candidates.label: Comoe

candidates.label: Antitrust Claims
candidates.definition: Antitrust Claims refer to legal claims made against individuals or companies for engaging in anti-competitive behavior, such as market allocation, vertical or horizontal agreements, pricing, or conspiracy.

candidates.label: Antitrust Efforts Provisions
candidates.definition: Antitrust Efforts Provisions refer to the specific provisions in a legal agreement that outline the standards and limitations for the parties' efforts to comply with antitrust laws and regulations.
synonyms: Dispositions de lutte contre les pratiques anticoncurrentielles
独占禁止法の取り組み規定

candidates.label: Antitrust - Horizontal Claims
candidates.definition: Antitrust - Horizontal Claims refer to legal claims made against companies that engage in anti-competitive behavior with their direct competitors, such as price-fixing or market allocation.
synonyms: 独占禁止法 - 横断的な請求

candidates.label: Antitrust Representation Language
candidates.definition: Antitrust Language refers to provisions in merger and acquisition agreements that address compliance with antitrust and competition laws. These clauses ensure that the proposed transaction does not result in anti-competitive practices or market concentration that would violate regulatory standards. They may also stipulate conditions or steps to be taken if regulatory approval is required or if there are potential antitrust concerns, such as divestitures or behavioral commitments.

candidates.label: Antitrust - Pricing Claims
candidates.definition: Antitrust - Pricing Claims refer to allegations of anti-competitive behavior related to pricing practices, such as price-fixing or predatory pricing, in violation of antitrust laws.
synonyms: 独占禁止法 - 価格請求

candidates.label: Antitrust - Vertical Claims
candidates.definition: Antitrust - Vertical Claims refer to legal claims related to antitrust violations that involve vertical relationships between parties, such as price fixing, market allocation, and tying arrangements.
synonyms: 独占禁止法 - 垂直クレーム

candidates.label: Antitrust - Tying Claims
candidates.definition: A tying claim in antitrust law refers to a situation where a seller requires a buyer to purchase a second product or service as a condition of purchasing the first product or service, which may be considered anti-competitive behavior.
synonyms: 独占禁止法 - 結合主張

candidates.label: Compliance
candidates.definition: Deontic compliance refers to the conformity in action, behavior, or attitude to a set of rules or standards, as prescribed by laws, regulations, or ethical principles.

candidates.label: Covenant Not to Compete Clause
candidates.definition: A Covenant Not to Compete Clause, also known as a Noncompete Clause, is a contractual clause that prohibits an individual from engaging in certain competitive activities with another party for a specified period of time and within a specific geographic area.
synonyms: वादा नहीं करता कि प्रतिस्पर्धा क्लॉज

candidates.label: Breach of Covenant Not to Compete
candidates.definition: The Breach of Covenant Not to Compete refers to a violation of an agreement between an employee and employer or principal, where the employee agrees not to engage in competitive activities with the employer or principal after leaving their employment or business relationship.
synonyms: Breach of Covenant Not to Compete
Incumplimiento de convenio de no competencia
Violação da obrigação de não concorrência

candidates.label: Regulatory Compliance
candidates.definition: The process by which an entity (i.e., organization or person) ensures that it observes and complies with the external statutory laws and regulations.
synonyms: 規制コンプライアンス

candidates.label: Litigation Practice
candidates.definition: Matters that include official proceedings (e.g., court case, arbitration, mediation)

candidates.label: Pretrial Practice
candidates.definition: Pretrial Practice refers to the legal work and procedures that take place before a trial, including but not limited to motions, pleadings, and discovery.

candidates.label: Tax Practice
candidates.definition: Tax practice refers lawyers assisting individuals or organizations in navigating tax laws, including compliance, filing returns, and handling disputes with tax authorities. These lawyers may also advise on tax planning strategies to optimize tax liability and benefit

candidates.label: Acquiree
candidates.definition: An acquiree is a company or entity that is being acquired or purchased in a merger or acquisition transaction. The acquiree is also known as the target firm during a takeover scenario.

candidates.label: Mergers and Acquisitions Law
candidates.definition: Law dealing with companies that are purchasing and/or merging with other companies.

candidates.label: Hosting Agreement
candidates.definition: A Hosting Agreement is a legally binding contract between a host and a guest, outlining the terms and conditions of the hosting arrangement, such as payment, duration, and responsibilities.

candidates.label: Merger Agreement
candidates.definition: A Merger Agreement is a legal document that outlines the terms and conditions of the merger between two or more companies.

hierarchy_path.label: Document Collections by Areas of Law

candidates.label: Standstill Agreement
candidates.definition: A Standstill Agreement is a contract in which one party agrees not to take specific actions against another party for a set period. It's often used in business contexts where one company agrees not to acquire more shares or make a takeover bid for another company for a specified duration.

candidates.label: War
candidates.definition: War and Conflicts refer to armed hostilities between states, nations, or groups, characterized by the use of military force and violence. These engagements can range from full-scale wars between countries to smaller conflicts such as civil wars, insurgencies, and guerrilla warfare, often resulting in significant human, economic, and political consequences.

candidates.label: Tak
candidates.definition: A province in Thailand located in the northern region of the country.

candidates.label: Hostile Takeover
candidates.definition: A hostile takeover occurs when the acquiring company attempts to take control of the target company without the consent or cooperation of the target company's management and board of directors. This is typically achieved through a tender offer directly to the shareholders or by launching a proxy fight to gain control of the board.

candidates.label: Deal Stance
candidates.definition: Deal stance refers to the approach taken by an acquiring company when pursuing a takeover of another company. They include hostile takeovers, friendly takeovers, proxy contests, control positions, and minority stakes.

candidates.label: Friendly Takeover
candidates.definition: A friendly takeover involves the acquiring company working in cooperation with the target company's management and board of directors to negotiate and agree upon the terms of the acquisition. In a friendly takeover, both parties collaborate to ensure a smooth transition and integration of the businesses.

candidates.label: Improperly Buying Securities During Corporate Takeover
candidates.definition: The concept of "Improperly Buying Securities During Corporate Takeover" refers to the act of purchasing securities in a company during a takeover in a manner that violates securities laws or regulations.

candidates.definition: A representation within a legal agreement that specifically pertains to the statutes and regulations related to the acquisition of a company.

candidates.definition: A "Superior Offer, or Acquisition Proposal reasonably likely/expected to result in a Superior Offer" is a proposal made during a legal matter that is expected to be better than any other offers made, and triggers an exception to a previously agreed upon no-shop covenant.

candidates.label: Merger

candidates.label: M&A Transactional Method
candidates.definition: A M&A Deal Structure is the specific plan or arrangement for the merger or acquisition of companies, including the methods used to acquire the target company, such as tender offers or mergers, and the legal framework supporting the execution of the plan.
synonyms: M&Aトランザクション手法

candidates.label: Usurpation of Corporate Opportunity
candidates.definition: The Usurpation of Corporate Opportunity is a claim made against a corporation or partnership for taking advantage of a business opportunity that rightfully belongs to the company, thereby depriving the company of the opportunity to pursue it.
synonyms: 企業機会の不正な奪取

candidates.label: Threatened
candidates.definition: A modifier of a criminal claim indicating that harm or danger has been communicated or perceived, and that action may be taken to prevent it.

candidates.label: Privatization
candidates.definition: Privatization is the process of transferring ownership and management of a business, enterprise, or public service from the government to private individuals or entities. This often aims to improve efficiency, foster competition, and reduce public sector burden.
